claim: MI300X AMD GPUs beat H100 on flash attention kernels specifically because they have 192GB HBM (vs H100's 80GB) with higher memory bandwidth, reducing time spent on parallelism strategies and communication overlap
actionable: When deploying inference on MI300X, prioritize workloads with high memory parallelism and bandwidth requirements; non-dense transformers benefit most from the 192GB VRAM advantage.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: Kernel developers should write from hardware properties up (understanding MI300X specs first) rather than top-down through high-level frameworks like Triton—this gives full control over tensor materialization and avoiding unnecessary HBM fetches
actionable: For GPU kernel optimization, start with hardware spec whiteboarding: what's the memory bandwidth? What are register constraints? Then write in ROCm/CUDA, avoiding abstraction layers that hide optimization opportunities.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: The METR software engineering productivity study found developers felt 20% faster with AI but were 20% slower; Quentin was one of the few showing measurable speedup by avoiding the 'slot machine effect' of endlessly prompting and being disciplined about context length
actionable: When using AI coding assistants, timeboxing and context awareness matter more than prompting volume; treat it as tool augmentation, not offloading your thinking.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: Avoid cursor-like tools that manage context invisibly; prefer API-based direct access so you can see exactly what context is sent to the model and catch when you're exceeding the model's optimal context window
actionable: Use local LLM interfaces with explicit model selection and API access; write manual summaries of long conversations and start fresh chats to maintain context hygiene.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: Models are poorly trained on GPU kernel code (especially CUDA/ROCm/PTX) because high-performance kernels are rarely open-sourced; Triton kernels are publicly available but CUDA kernels are scarce, making model-generated kernels unreliable below Triton level
actionable: Do not rely on LLM-generated CUDA/ROCm kernels; if you need them, start with skeleton/template generation and implement the hot path yourself. Expect significant iteration.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: Low-level systems code is harder to debug when AI-generated because parallelism and hardware interactions are opaque; humans struggle to mentally map GPU kernel execution, making incorrect behavior hard to spot even when code looks plausible
actionable: For GPU/systems code, trust AI for high-level scaffolding and documentation only; review and test all low-level implementations directly, never assume correctness.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: Context rot is a real problem: after 5-10 conversation turns, model attention gets 'clogged' and confused; refresh by summarizing the previous conversation and starting a new thread with the summary as context
actionable: Limit AI conversations to 1-2 turns per chat; if you need more, summarize the prior discussion and start a fresh chat with the summary for fresh attention.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: Extended thinking models (O1 preview, O3) are a major jump for core academic/ML work; they excel at first-principles performance modeling and explaining low-level systems behavior, unlike earlier models
actionable: For low-level systems problems (kernel optimization, performance modeling), use extended thinking models; for skeleton work and documentation, use standard models.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: Claude 3.5 Sonnet marked a shift where documentation, unit tests, and scaffolding became reliably one-shot-able for the first time, improving developer productivity across these routine tasks
actionable: For documentation, test writing, and boilerplate, use Claude 3.5 Sonnet confidently; you can batch these as one-shot tasks without iteration.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: The workflow that worked best for Quentin: skeleton/setup by AI, core implementation by human (with AI pair programming for insights), debugging solo, then unit tests + docs as one-shot AI—this split avoids the 'slot machine' pitfall
actionable: Map tasks into AI-suitable (skeleton, docs) and human-core (implementation, debugging) buckets; use AI for pair programming on hard thinking, not as solo builder.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: High-focus tasks (80%+ mental effort) should not be context-switched; interleave low-focus tasks (10-20%) while high-focus tasks are blocked—this capacity management is critical to sustainable AI-assisted productivity
actionable: Batch high-focus work into focused blocks; use context switches to low-focus tasks (docs, tests) while waiting on blocking issues.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: Do not use AI to offload thinking; use it to enhance your thinking—if you don't understand the AI's output, you'll eventually lose quality evaluation ability and slow down by default
actionable: Require yourself to understand AI-generated code before accepting it; if you can't explain it, ask the model to simplify or rebuild from scratch.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: When hiring engineers, prioritize velocity and intellectual curiosity over seniority; people who love learning new things and solving hard problems naturally use AI well (augmentation), while specialists often offload thinking incorrectly (delegation)
actionable: Look for engineers with blogs/Twitter threads diving deep into recent problems; hire for demonstrated learning velocity over credential seniority.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower

claim: Model architecture design should account for inference hardware from the start—choose hidden dimensions with powers-of-two, sizes that map well to target GPU tensor cores and available kernels, to maximize inference efficiency across deployment targets
actionable: When training foundation models, design hidden dimensions as powers-of-two and match them to target inference hardware specs; this co-design multiplies inference efficiency.
source_title: How Zyphra went all-in on AMD + Why Devs feel faster with AI but are slower
